{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks about the purpose of an intensifying screen in extraoral radiographs. From the retrieved documents, particularly Document [5], it is stated that in screen film X-ray imaging, the image receptor cassette contains radiographic film between two intensifying screens. The intensifying screens are made of materials that emit light when exposed to X-rays, and this light interacts with the film emulsion to form a radiographic image. This process enhances the sensitivity of the film, allowing for a lower radiation dose to be used to produce a diagnostic image. Additionally, the document notes that the use of intensifying screens increases contrast by improving the difference in signal between tissues. This is supported by Document [18], which explains that radiographic contrast is generated by differential attenuation and that intensifying screens contribute to contrast through their interaction with the film. While collimation (option D) is a separate function of lead plates to limit the X-ray field, it is not the role of the intensifying screen. Options A and C are incorrect because the intensifying screen does not directly decrease radiation to the patient (though it enables lower radiation use indirectly), and it does not decrease contrast. The primary function is to increase contrast by enhancing the light signal on the film. Therefore, the correct answer is B.", "answer_choice": "B"}
